output: Thank you, Mr. President. Mr. President, Madam Vice-President, the Commission gives the impression of acting like a distracted student who chases after lost time before handing in their assignment.
Certainly, the social package exists. But it is incomplete and can only be considered as a step towards a consideration of social issues by the Commission.

The President of the Commission did not take the full measure of the warning given by citizens in France, the Netherlands, and Ireland, and which would unfortunately have been identical elsewhere in the event of a popular vote.
These elections are calls to order.
If Europeans want more Europe, it is to build a societal project and not just to adopt even more technical directives.
The regulation concerning the internal market is certainly necessary, but it is not, by far, the most important thing.
The 2009 program, like its predecessors for that matter, will miss the point.
The citizens do not expect the commission to merely complete or even simplify the legislation.
They expect the commission to be equal to the task assigned to it by the treaties, the locomotive, the think tank of the Union.
It is not enough for this that its president be wise and silent, standing at attention behind the President of the Council.
We expect the Commission to act as a key player in the reflection on the nature of the societal project that we need.
But what is the societal project in this program?
The 2009 program does not contain serious errors, but it is not the program that our fellow citizens need.
Mr. President, it will be up to the new college to take its responsibilities.
I only hope that he will be less deaf to the calls of the peoples than the current one.
Thank you, Mr. President. First of all, a question to the Council.

what about the high-level conference on Georgia that had been proposed by the French presidency, and how does the current presidency of the Union intend to defend the territorial integrity of Georgia, while a country recognizes two separatist regions, Abkhazia and South Ossetia?
But it's a pity that Mr. Barroso is not here because in his speech, he was not convincing, perhaps because he himself is not convinced by the proposals that he made to us.
I think that the financial and economic situation is much more serious than he described it.
Even today, I believe that those who follow the internet clearly see that the stock markets continue to collapse despite the interventions.

So for me, it's truly the collapse of the current system, a completely obsolete system, the system that led to the failure, which led to land being totally exploited and suffocated by greenhouse gas emissions, which led to underdevelopment in the South, and which led to underdevelopment in our cities.
I thank the President, Commissioner. The current financial crisis clearly shows that when the problem is global, a global response is needed.
And this is true, especially in the field of agriculture or in the food sector.
This is why we notably created the World Trade Organization, on which we agree, which seeks to have globalized rules.

It so happens that it doesn't quite work as we would like, because we are facing the following problem: We need to reconcile two things. Free trade, which is necessary and on which everyone agrees, but also protections, which are just as necessary.
and the protection of economies or national agriculture, that does not mean protectionism.
To this end, the path we have taken for 60 years, since the GATT, is an unbalanced path that entails the reduction of customs duties with the aim of their elimination.
It turns out that, technically, the reduction of customs duties
It's difficult. The proof is a whole series of mathematical formulas, the South Korean formula, the European formula, or the best-known formula, the Swiss formula.
That doesn't really work because a product is not unitary.
It is composed of numerous elements with very different rules of origin, and technically reducing customs duties is not as simple as we think.
So we find ourselves in a technical impasse. And we are politically trying, through negotiations, to get out of this impasse.

However, it turns out that technologically, scientists have invented a new customs technology. The technology of deductible customs duties. Deductible by the exporter from the importing state's economy.
Concretely, this deductible customs duty transforms into a customs credit equal to the amount of the customs duty that the exporter bore in the importing country.
This customs credit has three characteristics: it is refundable, negotiable, and eligible for a bonus.
It can be reimbursed at the moment when the exporter buys from the importer. He can deduct the customs duty that he paid.
It is negotiable because if the exporting company, which bore 100 in customs duties, has nothing to buy from the importer, it negotiates its customs credit on the stock market or at the bank.
And it can be increased, if we want to help developing countries, the exporter, the importer, to offer a customs credit amount higher than the amount of customs duties.
Well, with this technique, we create an international trade currency whose money supply is equal to the amount of existing customs duties.
For example, the European Union would offer 13 billion euros in international currency in the commercial domain.

Thank you, Mr. President,
The Russo-Georgian conflict, the financial crisis, have been an opportunity for the European Union to assert itself on the world stage as a full-fledged political actor and of the first rank.
Thanks to your determination, Mr. President of the Council, Europe has managed to unite and find coordinated, effective, and rapid responses.
Faced with crises, Europe proves its existence and demonstrates its added value.
Regarding immigration, for example, the every-man-for-himself approach was no longer appropriate.
the adoption by the European Council of the pact on immigration and asylum, initiated by the French presidency, constitutes a considerable advance.
on the fight against climate change, Europe must set concrete objectives and show the way to its global partners.

You have already expressed yourself on this subject, Mr. President of the Council, but we wish for this agreement to be as balanced as possible, because it must take into account the economic situation of our countries, which is very destabilized currently.
But also to ask ourselves the question of the type of development we will choose for the future.
To meet the challenges it faces, the European Union needs the Treaty of Lisbon more than ever.
We understand the concerns of our Irish friends, but the status quo is not an option.
Ireland must offer us a solution at the December European Council. Many of us are demanding it.
In recent weeks, we have observed with pride that a strong political Europe, united in adversity, determined when facing its partners, and listened to on the international stage,
We wish that this new European mentality becomes the rule and endures.
The Treaty of Lisbon is the best solution we had to achieve it. Thank you.

Thank you, Madam President, Mr. Minister, I was in Tbilisi when President Sarkozy came to negotiate the peace plan. And of course, I pay tribute to him for having acted quickly and for having managed to stop this war.
But this war is somewhat our failure. We proved hesitant for 14 years. We silently witnessed the escalation of provocations in the separatist regions.

And this war has certainly awakened Europe, confronting it with its responsibilities. But the fire continues to smolder in the Caucasus, and we must make every effort to definitively resolve the frozen conflicts for the security of all of Europe.
And I also know the divisions, Mr. Minister, among European countries regarding Georgia's entry into NATO. And personally, I am against it.
And I will make a proposal. I will ask the European Union to propose the status of neutrality for these countries of the Caucasus.
And only the status of neutrality will be able to appease tensions with Russia and definitively prevent this sub-region from new conflicts.
This status will ensure the security of these young democracies and will contribute to our own security.
Commissioner, Mr. Minister, these last three days the European Development Days were held, where the food crisis was extensively discussed, a crisis that severely affects the poorest countries.
All interventions emphasized the absolute necessity for developing countries to develop their own agriculture, by optimally utilizing their land and natural resources.

Incidentally, the lyrical outbursts of those who called yesterday and still today for the reduction of state intervention and public spending, for all-out liberalization, for privatization, for the relegation of agriculture as
a virtually prehistoric activity. This U-turn, therefore, in favor of massive public investment in agricultural production and food sovereignty, all this with something both comical and scandalous.
The future of agriculture in developing countries has a direct relationship with the debate we are having today.

I unfortunately do not believe that this CAP health check, initiated before the paroxysm of the food riots and the disastrous market disorders, takes into account the situation of the peasantries of the South and fully addresses the consequences of the serious crises we are experiencing today: ecological, social, economic, and financial.

I therefore deeply regret that the strong relationship between the agriculture of the North and the South is so little addressed, both in the proposals of the Commission and in the report of the Parliament. And the calling into question of the billion euros promised shows how long the road still is.
Mr. President, The report we are going to vote on tomorrow marks the culmination of a year of work and negotiation, dedicated to the future of the Common Agricultural Policy and our farmers.
Indeed, it was last November 20th that Mrs. Fisher Ball came to present us with her proposals on the health assessment.
Following Lutz Goebel's progress report, we now have Kapoula Santos's report on the Commission's proposal.
We must congratulate our two colleagues for their excellent work.
The European Parliament's Committee on Agriculture has remained firm against the Commission's initial proposals, which we considered far too liberal.
I will remain vigilant concerning the dairy sector, and in particular the measures preceding the abandonment of quotas.

I am thinking particularly of mountain milk producers, who must not be the poor relations of this reform, and we must take care not to leave them aside, as they will need to be supported more than others.
I do not wish to play Cassandra, but how can one imagine the French dairy industry regrouped within a single region?
It is therefore with goodwill that I welcome the proposal to set up a milk fund, and the one that aims to evaluate, as early as 2010, the increase in milk quotas based on market developments.

The calculation of the progressive modulation carried out by the Agriculture Commission seems fair to me, and would allow us to strengthen the development of territories, rurality, and to sustainably preserve biodiversity.
I also commend the clarification of eco-conditionality, the implementation of which had until now been a real headache for farmers.

00:00 I also wish that we keep the amount of the tobacco fund, which allows us to preserve our small farms in Europe, as well as a significant workforce in a region where nothing else is exploitable and which allows us to avoid imported products.
Finally, Mr. Minister, we greatly appreciated your commitment throughout these negotiations.
You have demonstrated great patience and great determination, and you certainly haven't spared your time.
It was a real pleasure to work alongside you.
What you would have had with us was of great effectiveness.
I know that we could...
President, thank you first for the work accomplished and for the conclusions and orientations proposed by the CAP Health Check, from Mr. K. Santos' report.
Agriculture is indeed an economic sector that cannot respond to the simple signals of the market.
This activity has impacts, of course, on food self-sufficiency, but also, which is too often forgotten, on territories and on employment.
The suppression of market instruments would be a serious blow to our agriculture.

Decoupling, for example, constitutes a real difficulty for certain livestock farming territories, suckler cows and sheep, and I am delighted to have heard Madam Fischerboll just now speak on this question.
Furthermore, it is clear that the strict retention of historical references would put an enormous brake on any possibility of reform.
However, I still believe that the question of the second pillar of the CAP is an essential question because rural areas need to rely on agricultural activity to develop employment in rural areas.
And so, we must reflect on this second P issue, without, of course, amputating the first.
Given that Minister Jouyet will be leaving us, I believe that this Parliament should thank him.
He was one of the most assiduous ministers we have known.
I wish him good luck.

Madam President, I voted for the Parris report concerning the integration of the OCM 20 into the single CMO. I must say with a heavy heart, because I consider that this single CMO is not a simplification and does not increase transparency.
It will make life more complicated for viticulture and for the entire wine industry.
Last night, the commissioner tried to reassure us.

I hope that the Commission will keep its word and that, above all, the profession will still be adequately represented on the consultative committee, as has been the case since the first CMO. Thank you.
You are a professional.
Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues. Our group enthusiastically supports the Small Business Act proposal that the Commission presented to us last June.

Certainly, since the 2000s, initiatives have been taken in the direction of SMEs. And I am thinking in particular of the excellent work of our colleague Dominique Vlasto on the SME charter. Initiatives that have been appreciated but judged insufficient because they too often remained at the stage of pious hopes.
Today we must go further.
The severe economic crisis that our countries are experiencing, and whose consequences are particularly concerning for SMEs, invites us to do so if need be.
In this regard, the Small Business Act is part of a new, resolutely proactive approach.
In a few weeks, we will examine Mrs. Herzog's report on the communication of the Commission. But today, through the submission of this oral question, we wished to challenge the Council.
Mr. Minister, we have taken note with interest of the conclusions adopted yesterday by the Competitiveness Council, and we would like to know if the European Council on 11 and 12 December next,

will properly convey its conclusions and will provide a strong impetus so that the major guiding principle, Sink Small First, is rapidly transposed within the regulatory framework of the member states.
We wish that the council commits to the principle of the annexed action plan, accompanied by concrete measures.
In this regard, and in the short time allotted to me, I will emphasize three directions in which we need compelling impulses.
The impact studies, which should be systematic, and regular follow-up, with the control of the European Parliament, on the state of application of the Small Business Act in the Member States.
Access to public procurement.
The code of good conduct has no binding force; we must, together, be more imaginative, more inventive, and propose concrete measures that genuinely open access to these markets for SMEs.
Finally, access to financing, more indispensable than ever in the current context.
Your proposals are good, Mr. Minister, they must also integrate support for supply.
There is urgency.
Thank you for assuring us of the council's will.

Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Commissioner, we all know that SMEs are the backbone of the European Union, as they employ more than 70% of the European Union's workforce and represent 99% of European businesses.

And as has already been said, these millions of small and medium-sized businesses are now worried and threatened by the financial crisis because it degrades purchasing power and household confidence, impacting order books.

It is therefore the cash flow of SMEs that is in peril, and consequently, it is necessary to ensure their activities are supported by making sure that banks guarantee effective access to the financing required for the continuity of their operations.
This is the whole point of the conclusions of the Competitiveness Council of 1st December, which the Minister spoke about, and of the recovery plan approved by the European finance ministers.
Making the needs of SMEs a permanent political priority for the Union is essential. And I wanted to commend the action plan in favour of the Small Business Act for Europe.
It indeed reflects the will to ensure the effective application of the guiding principle, giving priority to small businesses first, that is to say, to European SMEs.
However, it is necessary, as the commissioner said, and as we all say, that the Council truly engages with the other institutions.
European, so that this principle is now made effective, we must go beyond mere declarations; we must move to implementation in all European policies and programs.
However, we can say that this action plan is a real response to the urgency of the situation.
And I wanted, Mr. Minister, to commend the proactive approach of the French presidency of the Union, which quickly enabled intentions to be translated into action.

Mr. President, Madam President-in-Office, Mr. Commissioner,
I give my full support to the rapporteur, who is defending the position that was ours at first reading, a position rejected by the governments of the Member States.
It was urgent to bring our legislation on on-call time into conformity with the case law of the Court of Justice. This has been done.
the report provides balanced and protective responses for employees.
All on-call time is counted as working time.
Compensatory rest occurs immediately after the period of service.
It is a matter of common sense to guarantee decent working conditions, especially for medical professions.
But the reform of the Working Time Directive also gives us the opportunity to advance our European social law by abolishing the individual opt-out.
The Circass report seizes this opportunity and proposes a progressive abolition of any possibility to derogate from the maximum legal working time.
We must face reality.
It is a fallacy to say that the employee is in an equal position with their employer and can refuse what is offered to them.
Dear colleagues, we must clearly show the government of the Member States that the text it wishes to impose on us is unacceptable.

And in the chorus of praise that will certainly lull the ears of the French government as early as tomorrow, it seems necessary to me to first and foremost focus on the employees who will be called upon to work more and more, without truly having the choice, and this, like all those who will be asked tomorrow to work on Sunday in France.
It is moreover clearly to open up this possibility that the French government changed its stance on the individual opt-out in the Council.

My dear colleagues, let's listen to the employees and try to answer their call. If we don't want the negative votes of the French, Dutch and Irish referendums to be followed by many others, calling into question a European Union that would give them the feeling of not addressing their daily problems. Thank you.

Thank you, Mr. President.
It was a very good French presidency.
There were very few dissenting voices not to agree with it.
The 27 set aside their differences to reach a series of decisions that were exemplary.
On the energy-climate package, Europe is taking a first step, and in that, this first step is immense.
It now has the means to assume its environmental responsibility without, however, sacrificing its economy.
She can henceforth take the leadership in this matter, to lead the other continents on the path of sustainable development.
Moreover, Ireland proposes a scenario to emerge from its isolation.
Our Irish friends will once again express their views on the Lisbon Treaty, with all the cards in hand, since practically all the other members will have definitively made their decision.
The current crises demonstrate to us that the Union more than ever needs the best institutional tools to be effective.
The European Council has adopted a massive and coordinated economic recovery plan, based on long-term productive investment and targeted support for the hardest-hit industrial sectors.
President Sarkozy raised the issue of reducing certain VAT rates, and I completely share his point of view.
Let's hope that the ministers of economy and finance will reach an agreement in March, which appeared impossible to the heads of state and government last weekend.
The challenges that Europeans face give the Union the opportunity to reveal itself in their eyes as more united, more responsive, and more concerned with protecting them.
We are grateful to the French presidency for having instilled this new dynamic.
We hope, like Nicolas Sarkozy, not to see again anytime soon yesterday's councils, which ended at 4 AM to negotiate three peanuts.
Over the last six months, Europe has indeed changed its mindset, its scope, its credibility.
We must thank the French presidency. We are well aware that it is not entirely foreign to it. Thank you, Mr. President.
The situation in Gaza is known to everyone.
that we discuss here in Parliament is natural.

But we demand that the Security Council takes a stand. We demand that the institutions take a stand, that the European Union takes a stand. And we in Parliament, we discuss and we don't want to take a stand.

I find that, nevertheless, faced with the urgency of the situation in Gaza, it is of extreme importance that this Parliament speaks out and says exactly what it wants to stop the massacres that are taking place in the Middle East right now.
I find it inadmissible that this parliament does not have the courage and lucidity to vote on a resolution after our debate.
That is why our group asks to revise the decision of the Conference of Presidents and to conclude the debate that we have, that we must
to have on the situation in Gaza, through a resolution that will demonstrate the clear and firm position of this parliament, of a majority, so that the massacres in Gaza stop.

We want a resolution. We must be aware that we have a political responsibility for what is happening, and this political responsibility cannot simply be a discussion. It must be a resolution that clearly states what we want and what we denounce.
You're welcome. Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, more than ever, the directive proposal that Parliament is about to vote on evokes in me a very mixed feeling.
It is true that the rapporteur Mr. Klinz has been carrying out a sustained effort for years and that he negotiated skillfully, particularly during the trilogue.

It is also true that the directive introduces a number of significant advances, contributing to a better functioning of the internal market and the collective management of financial assets, and is welcomed as such by the European investment fund industry.

It remains that the debates and discussions in the Council and in Parliament have never been entirely free, since the result was known in advance. For certain actors, it was necessary at all costs to introduce the European passport for management companies.
On principle, there is nothing to criticize; the passport is perfectly defensible. However, it is still necessary to ensure that the introduction of the passport for management companies,
is accompanied by the necessary guarantees, particularly regarding fund supervision, since it leads to a dissociation of functions beyond borders.
However, I note with regret that the system implemented is not only complex, but is subject to divergent interpretations.
He risks very quickly encountering practical difficulties that will harm the European industry, particularly with regard to exports to third countries.
I have duly noted my minority position; nevertheless, I do not feel uncomfortable.
In normal times, I would have been tempted to say that this practice will allow for drawing definitive conclusions on the matter and thus to determine
who is right, between those who trust the adaptive capacities of market players and those who stick to a more cautious approach.
It is not out of the question that success will be achieved, but on the other hand, nothing guarantees it either.
Now, very recent news, particularly the Madoff scandal and its implications for the collective financial asset management industry, is not designed to reassure us.
Skepticism succeeds my concern.
The investment fund industry will not be spared by the financial crisis; we are already observing this.
It may be that fundamental re-evaluations are necessary and that from this crisis context...

that in this context of deep crisis, we would make choices that dilute the responsibilities of the actors or which presuppose a perfect cooperation between control authorities can truly be considered an incongruity.
I tell you as I think it, this directive is also that of a bygone period from before the crisis.
It is characterized by a certain lightheartedness.
We have made the financial system will go through a crisis of confidence, lasting and fundamental, depends entirely on its activity, on its architecture, must be rethought.

Let's not forget, the primary function of UCITS is investor protection, not risk diversification and the establishment of strict rules. By voting on this text as it stands, without really knowing where we are going, we are acting as if nothing, absolutely nothing, had happened.
This autism bodes nothing good, that is why I will abstain, even though I signed the compromise amendments negotiated with the Council.
Given the circumstances, the improvements made seem, in fact, to be of little weight compared to the questions of principle I have just explained.

Mr. President,
Although the regulation on animal reporting has been in force since January 2007, it seems that member states are not systematically in compliance, by providing, as they were asked, annual reports.
Also, does a global analysis remain random, since many elements are missing concerning the means put in place to conduct inspections?
As a member of an animal protection association and a member of the Intergroup on Animal Welfare, this subject is of particular concern to me.
Associations have been fighting desperately for decades, so that only finally in 2007 were directives established in this area, where we see that member states display a certain laxity.
I would even go so far as to say a certain ill will, because, after all, as we know, the implementation of control and inspection is absolutely not insurmountable.
We know the slaughterhouses, we know the breeding grounds, we know the transit routes for livestock. So, where is the problem?

I believe it is important in our time, where public opinion is increasingly aware, and rightly so, of animal welfare, whether it concerns the conditions of breeding, slaughter, or transport, that states respect these sentiments.
And since I have the floor, I would like to add, regarding transport, that it would be essential to consider and take into account, independently of transport durations, local climatic conditions.
Indeed, a multi-hour transport carried out in the Netherlands in spring is nothing comparable to the same transport time in mid-summer in a country like Greece.
Shouldn't we impose night transport in these latter cases?
I thank you for considering this proposal in the future.

Mr. President, I am delighted that it was a Briton, our colleague Parish, who tabled this text, as if the Roman soldiers, 2000 years ago, did well to go to Great Britain and bring amphorae of olive oil and wine.
But then, two consequences will have to be drawn from this.

It's not very reasonable symbolically to put the food agency in Helsinki. And it is not reasonable that the European Commission does not go back on the following points. First, to restore aid for almond trees. Then, to stop the

the aggressions that are committed against olive trees, especially in Andalusia and throughout Europe. Stop the aggressions committed against red tuna fishermen in the Mediterranean, and the aggressions committed against the European vineyard with the uprooting.
I suggest to our Commissioner, to our President Mr Barroso, that we organize a grand banquet in Portugal, with sheep's cheese and wine.
Yes, Mr. President, we have all indeed campaigned for the closure of Guantanamo, and we all welcomed President Obama's decision to close this prison of shame.
Today, the question of hosting Guantanamo detainees should not arise for us.
As Europeans, faithful to our values of defending human rights, we must take our responsibilities, because it is our duty to welcome these former detainees.
And one could read – and here I address the Council – one could read in the press that member states were not very keen on this reception.
Therefore, I would really like to launch an appeal to the Council and, in particular, to the reluctant member states, so that they agree to receive these detainees on their territory.

And I would tell you, dear colleagues, that the worst would be if the beautiful momentum generated by the closure of Guantanamo were to get bogged down because we would not have answered the call when Europe was appealed to.

important, because the European animal feed sector constitutes one of the most important sectors of agriculture, both in terms of production, as it represents half of the agricultural production in the European Union, 120 million tons, and in terms of turnover, approximately 50 billion euros.
The European Union indeed has 5 million animal breeders and 60 million households owning pets.

In the past, the European Union has had to manage a number of health crises, which means that today we must be more vigilant regarding transparency to meet the expectations of breeders, but also consumers.

The provisions set out in this report have the advantage of representing a minimal cost for industries and a great benefit for consumers who are increasingly attentive to the quality of the products they buy.

The adoption of this report will make it possible to limit risks by guaranteeing a better quality of products, better control, improved traceability, and comprehensive information for the farmer, and thus ultimately, for the consumer.
Today, with the increase in international trade, it is absolutely imperative to strengthen all prevention mechanisms so that the food crises we have experienced in the past do not recur.

This report manages to reconcile the right to information, while strictly defining the nutritional elements that must appear on labels, and the right to intellectual property, so important for preserving the competitiveness of our industries.

I believe that manufacturers should indeed be obliged to immediately notify the use of any new raw material entering into the composition of animal feed, in order to ensure transparency and facilitate the control carried out by the competent authorities.
The inclusion of an emergency procedure that allows for the introduction of a new dangerous substance into the list of prohibited materials seems to me absolutely indispensable.

Similarly, allowing the breeder to question the competent national authority or the European Commission in case of doubt about an unfair allegation allows for better verification of the system and for protecting the client while preserving the fairness of commercial transactions.

I therefore wish to give my full support to this excellent report, because experience shows that regulation on the labeling of animal feed – I'll finish, Mr. President – favouring quality, transparency, traceability, and control, is the best guarantee to prevent new health crises in Europe.

President, ladies and gentlemen,

This is certainly not Europe, much less its High Representative for Foreign Policy, Javier Solana, former Secretary General of NATO, who can play the role of mediator between Israel and the Palestinians.
At most, they are called upon to finance the reconstruction of the Gaza Strip, as they do today for Kosovo, Lebanon, or Afghanistan.
The Americans and Israelis are bombing, the Europeans are financing the reconstruction.
What is the distribution of tasks among allies?
However, it should be the vandals who pay.
Egypt is at the center of peace talks concerning a prolonged ceasefire with Hamas.

But the challenge to be met is formidable, insofar as the new Israeli government, under pressure from the third man, Lieberman, leader of an extreme right that is democratically accepted there, risks seeing its task singularly complicated in this negotiation.
In fact, Netanyahu, who is presented as the future Prime Minister, has always opposed a truce with Hamas.
Another difficulty. The Palestinian Authority of Mahmoud Abbas has become in the West Bank a kind of international protectorate whose legitimacy has greatly diminished among the population.
Last element to take into account, the expansion of Israeli settlements, which has never ceased since 1967, makes the creation of a Palestinian state in the West Bank particularly delicate.
Today, the ball is in the Israeli court. But do the hawks in both camps accept this truce of God that both parties demand without giving themselves the means to achieve it?
Allow me to add a thought regarding France's reintegration into NATO's integrated military structure, which will be debated with the Vatanen report.
This reintegration will entail severe obligations for France.
Indeed, we are rejoining NATO while the Cold War has been over since 1990.
Sarkozy seems to have forgotten the fall of the Berlin Wall and the return of Russia to the ranks of free nations.
Should we strengthen the logic of blocks at a time when multipolarization and the rise in power of emerging countries, including militarily,
Furthermore, France's vision of an integrated structure will force it to reinforce its contingent in Afghanistan, even though it already has 3,300 men on the ground.
With what budget will it finance, while its defense budget will fall below 2% of GDP, and more than 30 regiments will be abolished.
Paradoxically, we will increase our budgetary participation in order to integrate NATO and at the same time reduce our military presence in Africa.
The European defense, dear to President Sarkozy, will therefore be a pillar of the Atlantic Alliance.
One only had to read the Treaty of Lisbon and its additional protocols to be convinced of it.
Whether it be in terms of foreign policy or common security, the European path is a dead end that will only lead to an alignment with the Americans and their allies.
It is this logic of erasure that we reject in the name of sovereignty and national independence, relying in particular on our autonomous nuclear deterrence force.
Thank you, Madam President. I congratulate our three rapporteurs for this rather remarkable synthesis they have produced of the current state of our common foreign and security policy.

Mr. Representative, you know it better than anyone else. Europe is awaited today on the world stage to take the place assigned to it by its economic and demographic power, and the richness of its democratic and humanist values.

We must acknowledge that despite the will regularly expressed by more than two-thirds of our European fellow citizens, as you said, certain progress has been made – I grant you that – over the last 10 years, but we must acknowledge that this Europe still does not exist.
Should we need a recent illustration of its disappearance in the solution of the new drama in the Near East, it would be there to demonstrate it.
There was, and still is, a need for a European presence in Gaza to play an active role there, both in helping the survival of the populations and the reconstruction of the
of the country, and in the fight against arms smuggling, which had made it possible to transform this territory into a launch base for rockets of all calibers.
Despite the good words lavished in Sharm el-Sheikh and Jerusalem, nothing has yet been done in this regard.

I am reiterating a question already asked during the Lebanese crisis: when will we be able, Mr. High Representative, to hope for the deployment of a European fleet in the Mediterranean, just as we deployed one in response to piracy?
We have the means to do so.
Will we one day have the will?
Yes. Mr. Commissioner, Mr. President, dear colleagues, the Parliament's decision to accept the urgency requested by the Council for the
the handling of this file was unanimous, both at the Fisheries Committee last week and this morning right here in plenary.
Uh, we are all indeed aware of the need for the European Union to honor the commitments made in Marrakech last November by the International competent Commission, the ICCAT.
Specifically, it involves implementing the recommendations adopted by this organization, in order to curb the persistent phenomenon of bluefin tuna overfishing.
East Atlantic and in the Mediterranean and to face the serious dangers that arise from it for the future of the species and therefore that of the fishery itself.
Two of our colleagues, from the Committee on Fisheries, Madam Fraga, Mr. Romeva Rueda, were present in Marrakech and will speak on this subject in the context of this debate.
I would like, for my part, to recall the importance that our commission attaches to this file, which involves the main dimensions of the common fisheries policy, that is to say, the management
of the resource, fleet management, respect for international, regional, and bilateral agreements, technical measures, and above all, and above all, control, I will come back to that shortly.
This characteristic dossier therefore serves as a test in our eyes of the credibility of this common fisheries policy.

In our view, it is not antinomic.
As a doctor, I would say that in the event of a fever, there is no point in blindly prescribing antipyretics without seeking to treat its cause.
Should these funds be allocated, it will be up to this state to put its finances in order, in order to bridge its excessive deficit and correct its past errors.

Just as a bank, interfering in an individual's private life and management, will make its personal loan conditional on a guarantee of solvency, similarly the European Union can lend to Ireland on the basis of consolidated solvency.
Without interfering in their private financial management, the bank will encourage the individual to negotiate, for example, a salary increase or to borrow elsewhere.

Thus, by respecting subsidiarity, the European Union, on the basis of a memorandum established with the IMF, considers that this solvency is achieved through the reduction of the legal minimum wage and an adjustment of pensions, without exempting itself from fiscal measures.
It is up to Ireland to choose whether or not to implement these measures.

This is not opening the door to interference by the European Union in the areas of subsidiarity, which would, for example, dangerously impose a minimum European wage level, but rather a protection of the stability of the Eurozone through a process of mutual guarantee.

Of course, it is appropriate for Ireland, like other countries in difficulty, which also protects us, but not at any price, especially that of seeing a state unable to repay in the long term, thus weakening its citizens and those of the entire European Union.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, the eruption of the Icelandic volcano last April, as many of us said earlier, provided a painful illustration of the fragmentation and the poor effectiveness of European airspace.
And each one of us in this hemicycle has been directly concerned,
and 5 million of our fellow citizens found themselves stranded without a real alternative transport solution.
And one year after this exceptional event, new European measures are still awaited.

A first crisis exercise, as you said, Commissioner, related to volcanic ash, is indeed scheduled for April 13th and 14th, under the coordination of Eurocontrol, ICAO, and IATA, but it is essential that EASA assumes real leadership for the coordination of security measures at the European level.
It is certain that the information provided by the VACs of London and Toulouse must be refined using more modern technological means.

These data, as we saw last April, can however be contradictory and can lead each Member State to take decisions concerning the closure of their airspace, less motivated by information than by emotion.
In this context, the role of EASA is essential for proposing a harmonized interpretation of this data, as well as for ensuring the centralization and proper dissemination of information.
Beyond the VAK information, EASA must implement a mechanism for collecting available data, by closely involving member states, airlines, and their pilots.
This centralization of information and the coordination of all concerned stakeholders will make it possible to avoid the chaos and inconsistencies we witnessed last year.
I therefore expect the Commission, together with EASA, to quickly present concrete proposals that will be fully integrated into the realization of the single European sky.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, this text is important.
It's not a revolution, we won't be able to say that everything was bad before and that everything is perfect now.
Nevertheless, it presents a number of major advances at this provisional stage of our work.
The main points relate, in my opinion, to better regulation of distance selling, particularly online. And this is chapter 3, because consumer rights must be strengthened in this area.
E-commerce is very useful. It broadens consumer choice, allows them to obtain the lowest prices, and it also allows small and medium-sized businesses and artisans to find new markets.
To advance in this area, it had to be done in a truly European way, that is to say, on a few very targeted points, to implement a complete harmonization of everyone's rights and duties.
Very concretely, an Italian customer buying on a Belgian website or a German website had to know that from now on, he would benefit from the same rights, the same protection,
This is what this directive will make possible.
This means that its withdrawal period will become 14 days throughout the Union.
This means that he will immediately know the total amount he will have to pay, without fearing an unpleasant surprise afterwards.
This means that thanks to the double click, he will correctly verify his order.
Obviously, all this will disrupt certain habits.
In many states, there are subtleties in national law that everyone is convinced are fundamental, whereas sometimes we don't even find a trace of them in neighboring countries.
But living together in Europe means learning to slightly modify one's habits in the service of the community and general interest.
Let's be clear, this text will still need to be improved, and we will have to do so in the coming weeks, with the Council and with the Commission.
And I appreciated the openings that have just been made in this regard.
But we have already done substantial work thanks to our rapporteur Andreas Schwab, whom I warmly thank.
But the real outcome of this work is above all the fact that the amendments will be voted on tomorrow by all political groups.
Consumer confidence in the Union's internal market is very fragile.
It required this political unanimity to preserve it and to hope to strengthen it.

Thank you, Madam President, dear colleagues, Commissioner.
The Open Skies agreement between Europe and the United States is a step forward, but it is only one step. A step which places Europe in a dilemma.

Either we accept an agreement that disadvantages European companies, or we suspend the agreement and risk losing the benefits acquired since 2008.

Allow me then to draw a parallel with the licenses of private and professional pilots. A text that will be discussed before the summer. Because pilots, of whom I am one, find themselves facing a dilemma.
The European Aviation Safety Agency, wishing to harmonize licenses across Europe, will do so to the detriment of the 80 to 90 thousand general aviation pilots,
directly concerned, some holding only one American license. And to the detriment, and this is important, of businesses and jobs linked to this activity.
I wish to draw your attention to this project, which has no security foundation.
There are 31 times fewer accidents among American license holders.
The objectives of basic regulation text 216/2008 allow for the recognition and equivalence of licenses, for which we see no point in waiting for hypothetical agreements postponed until 2014.

Thus, I propose a very open skies agreement to you and even invite you to accompany me when I sometimes come to Parliament, flying myself, to demonstrate to you that an American pilot's license is completely safe.
Thank you, Madam President. At this stage of our debate, I will necessarily be led to repeat or to support some of the things that have been said previously.
But I also wanted to say that, like several of my colleagues, the problems we are facing, which the entire European Union is currently facing, cannot be resolved by any single member state alone.
No Member State must find itself alone in the face of the challenge that confronts it, and which, once again, in my opinion, affects the entire European Union.
It is therefore truly to the European Union's capacity to organize itself and to cope in a common, truly united way that I appeal.
I believe the time has truly come now to lay the foundations for a genuine European policy on immigration and asylum.
More than a year ago, a European asylum and immigration pact had been adopted.
The time has now come, I think, to articulate it very concretely.
So we have indeed heard, Madam Commissioner, we have heard a number of recommendations that she made.

that we must strengthen the human resources, the material resources, the financial resources of the Frontex agency, to allow it to support more assertively the Hermes operations and the Poseidon operations.

and this will be the financial expression of our solidarity.

Madam Commissioner mentioned, I believe she went to Tunisia to talk about them, mobility partnerships.
Yes, these instruments must be strengthened in a spirit of responsibility, in a spirit of solidarity, but also in a precise and concrete spirit. Thank you.

Mr. Commissioner,
You undoubtedly know that the most intensive discussions we have had here around your communication concern the place of public services, services of general interest in the single market, and their legislative translation.
We have reached a compromise formulation which invites you to go beyond your proposal 25, and we ask you to go beyond a simple
communication that would not meet the expectations and needs of the actors, users, and all stakeholders of public services, and which would also not meet the objectives that you, moreover, formulate.
The European Union must convey a clear and unambiguous message on public services.
To do this, we must create a positive legal framework, a regulation or a directive.
The Treaty of Lisbon provides us with its legal bases.
It is now your turn to translate them into legislative proposals.
I think the parliament is ready to follow you in this direction.

Thank you, Mr. President.
Well, first of all, congratulations to all the rapporteurs for their colossal work on this discharge, and I also welcome the presence of the Council for this debate.
The seat of the European Parliament in Strasbourg is a question of European identity, and it is not merely an economic question.

I am speaking about it today in this chamber, on the occasion of the debate on the 2009 discharge, because the symbol of Franco-German reconciliation is treated by some members of the Kommen-man Budgetary Control Committee as a simple means of saving money.
The question of Parliament's spending interests me as well.
In this regard, I propose to hold the group weeks in Strasbourg, rather than in Brussels; this would allow us to make the most of our Parliament's seat in Strasbourg.
And since we want to make savings, I suggest other things, for example, to eliminate our colleagues' Friday per diem, also the elimination of per diems for journalists.
Europe is sufficiently attractive that one does not have to give per diem to journalists.

Let us not hide behind the budgetary argument to appropriate an important issue that goes beyond the scope of budgetary control, and this, even though I commend the important work of Ville, our rapporteur, concerning this discharge.
Finally, since we are talking about economics, I also wish that we moralize and clean up the compensation system for sponsored visitor groups.

I do not find it very moral, and it gives a very bad image of our institution, that the group leader be reimbursed in cash on the day of the visit, without any verification or control of what the group spent.
Sometimes the sum allocated, I should point out, exceeds the amount of the actual expenses.

It would be logical for groups to send a quote beforehand to the visits department, for them to receive an advance payment from Parliament for expenses, and that into a bank account, and for the balance to be paid upon presentation of invoices actually paid, with a ceiling, obviously. Thank you.
Thank you, Madam President. Madam, I am addressing the committee, my colleagues, I welcome the presence of the Council.
The question that arises today stems from the 2011 management of the Schengen Area.

This is no longer the space of the five member states of the 80s; on the contrary, it is a space that has constantly expanded in recent years and which today numbers 25 states, including three non-members of the European Union.
As with all European policies, Schengen cooperation must therefore adapt to the reality it covers today, which is no longer the same as it was ten years ago.

It is in this sense that we must read the proposals of the Italian and French governments, and the pragmatic and necessary responses provided today by the Commission in its communication on immigration.
Mutual trust between the States Parties to the Schengen area is certainly the cornerstone of Schengen cooperation.
But this trust rests on reciprocal obligations.
If one of the parties does not fulfill its obligations and creates vulnerabilities, the entire system becomes defective.
It must therefore be possible for other states to respond to this failing by re-establishing a temporary and strictly supervised control of its borders.
By doing so, failing states will clearly be invited to better control their external borders.

Furthermore, as presented by the Commission, these measures would fully respect the Schengen spirit, insofar as these controls would be reintroduced as a last resort, and the decision would be taken at the European level and not intergovernmental.
Such measures will be intrinsically linked to all measures envisaged long-term, in the context of a common migration policy.
It is also essential to strengthen Frontex, to provide for more solidarity—I conclude—to support the states situated at the external borders.
The situation experienced by the Mediterranean in recent years merely highlights a shortcoming of the Schengen acquis.
Madam President, for two minutes. In a few weeks, migrants coming from countries bordering the Mediterranean have accounted for a notable percentage of France's annual immigration.
This achievement is not static, but must on the contrary evolve.

I hope that these proposals will be quickly adopted in order to guarantee as quickly as possible better management of our Schengen area and thereby ensure the security of our common external borders. Thank you.

repression.

Madam Mathieu, one and a half minutes.
Thank you, Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues. First of all, I would like to congratulate my colleague Carlos Coelho, who did a goldsmith's job on this report, and God knows it was very complicated.
Bulgaria and Romania have made considerable efforts to strengthen control of their borders, and they have succeeded.
Whether it concerns police and judicial cooperation, data protection, visa policy, or the control of air, sea, or land borders, the latest assessments are very clear.
All technical criteria are met.
We can only note this state of affairs.
Bulgaria and Romania are now able to implement this Schengen acquis, both technically and in terms of personnel.
Furthermore, if no obstacle today prevents this enlargement of the Schengen area, we note that a number of recommendations have been addressed to these two countries.

I hope that these recommendations will be strictly followed, especially regarding the borders between Bulgaria, Greece, and Turkey, because as you know, mutual trust among the Schengen States relies on the full respect by these states of their reciprocal obligations.
If one of the parties fails to meet its obligations, it creates a vacuum, and the entire system becomes deficient.
I sincerely hope that an agreement will be reached by the Council to welcome these two countries into the privileged circle formed by the Schengen States.
And we all understand, in this chamber, the implications of this type of decision for the citizens concerned.
Once again, congratulations to Mr. Coelho for his report. Thank you, Mr. President.
Mr. President, in its Europe 2020 strategy, the European Union set itself the objective of making our economy greener and our transport more modern.
All European institutions have largely supported this objective, including almost all member states and our Parliament.
In this context, it is therefore regrettable that since 2008, several member states have endeavored to strip the new Eurovignette directive proposal of its substance and to block this file.

Today, however, thanks to the tenacity of our rapporteur—and I would like to acknowledge Saïd El Kadraoui here—thanks to his tenacity and that of the shadow rapporteurs, in their negotiation with the Council, we finally have a compromise before us.

So, certainly, this text is much less ambitious than the initial proposal, but it will allow for the first time to put into practice the polluter-pays principle and to internalize the external costs of transport, such as noise or CO2 emissions.

So that this e-vignette is not just a simple tax, but effectively contributes to the improvement of transport in Europe, member states are also encouraged to reinvest the funds collected in the modernization of transport modes.
The Parliament has also succeeded in obtaining concrete advances concerning the interoperability of toll systems, as well as stronger incentives for the use of less polluting heavy goods vehicles.

In order to remain consistent with the political objectives set by our institution, I sincerely hope that we will be able to adopt this compromise proposed to us in second reading, and thus pave the way for a more sustainable transport policy. Thank you.

Madam President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, yes, I agree that with liberty and fraternity, there should be equality with the farmers and the countries that have joined, with the countries joining from the East.
Yes, I agree with you.
I believe we have taken a big step forward with this report.
It wasn't easy at the start, and we were more in a confrontation, but we have reached a consensus, and that is the most important thing.
This consensus makes it possible to protect the global project, and I believe that this is the great merit of the European Parliament.
We succeeded in this challenge and it wasn't easy, and I therefore thank rapporteur Mercedes and all the parliamentarians.
First step.
In this text, we reaffirm the need for both equality, justice, and equity between producers and between territories, and I believe that is important.
We stated very clearly, and we confirmed, the maintenance and development of small farms, the consideration of small farms, which is essential for the quality and biodiversity of the territories.
I believe that it was also fundamental.
The fact that we introduced this notion of making agriculture agronomic, of turning the page on industrial, productivist agriculture, has been enshrined in the texts.
We will now have to implement this; it is a condition to make agriculture possible for future generations.
It's not an ideological question.
Finally, today, the concern we have is that there are indeed threats to the budget.
It will be necessary to answer it clearly.

What we are now waiting for is for us to effectively enter the second phase, which is clearly the legislative debate. Because beyond the texts and grand intentions, it is in the legislative debate that we will truly see where we are headed. And that is obviously what we are awaiting now.
I would nevertheless like to conclude by raising a cry of anger against Germany's attitude, which led to food aid being reduced fivefold for the poorest in Europe.
It was in the budget of the...
I want us to find the means to help the poorest. There are 80 million European citizens who do not eat properly to satisfy their hunger.

Thank you, Mr. President. While the European Parliament can boast of some good progress in terms of economic governance, I equally invite my colleagues to show humility, because we are dancing on the edge of the abyss.
Let's be well aware of two things.
The first is that the European Parliament remained inaudible in the Greek crisis.
While usually, we are so quick to react to all sorts of events with resolutions all over the world, this existential crisis seems to leave us speechless.
I deeply regret that we were unable to send a strong, concrete, simple message to the Greeks, to the governments of the member states, to European citizens.
The second observation concerns the scope of this package.
By voting, which I hope, for these six directives and/or regulations, we will certainly make the Eurozone accomplish a
nothing more, towards a discipline that we have missed so much, a step, I say, nothing more, which will show its usefulness in the future.
But one only has to see the heated discussions surrounding the overturned qualified majority to measure the distance we still have to cover. While it should be about automatic rules and sanctions.
The mechanism of the reversed qualified majority continues to act on some like a scarecrow.
We must nevertheless remain firm on the principle.
On a strictly political level, I regret that the left continues to reject the principles of sound budgetary management contrary to all evidence.
Yes, deficit and sustainable growth are antinomic.
Yes, states must also manage their budget like a good head of household. But no to stimulus through public spending. It's a sweet poison in the short term, but it's never a lasting solution.
While the coming weeks are set to be crucial, today it is a matter of refounding an economic union.
We voted this morning, after three years of work, on a very important text for the progress of consumer protection in Europe.
It rightly focuses on online purchases, which might perhaps in 20 years represent 25% of all trade in Europe, which is quite considerable.
These management systems will notably be cross-border.
They will allow consumers to broaden their choice and also to pay much less.
This is a real one.
For SMEs, for all artisans, it is also a very important opportunity to broaden their markets.

Among the main advances, I would just like to mention two. The withdrawal period, which is extended to 14 days. The summary of all information and the total price to pay before pressing the last button that places the order.

And finally, I am proud, in the context of these explanations of vote, to say that I, and indeed all of our colleagues, voted unanimously among all political groups. Because on such a subject, it was not controversy that we needed, but obviously consensus.
It is we who have invented it.

Madam, Mr. Commissioner,

ensuring good operational management of these databases is therefore a major issue to which it is our responsibility to offer an effective response.

This answer is, of course, very delicate, and Carlos Squalo had to face numerous legal, institutional, budgetary, political, and technical obstacles. And despite everything, he managed to arrive at a balanced text that satisfies all interested parties.
The creation of this network agency indeed seems to be the best solution to ensure the internal security of the member states.
No other existing institution or agency could today manage the proper management of these information systems.

It will allow for the improvement of the management of existing information systems, it will offer better synergy between its databases, and will also allow for the integration of instruments that will be adopted in the future.
One of the very delicate questions that arose concerning this file was the agency's headquarters. Where was it to be established?
Estonia and France are the only two countries that have put themselves forward as candidates to host it.
France already hosts the Schengen Information System in Strasbourg, for which it ensures operational management and administration since 1995, and it therefore has complete technical mastery of it.
Following the conclusions of the European Council of December 2003, which gave priority to the new member states, Estonia also wished to host the seat of this future agency.

It seems to me that the compromise reached is very good; a sort of distribution of missions—I'm concluding, Madam President—took place by transferring the agency's headquarters to Tallinn, where the tasks of governance, strategy, and program design will be carried out.
The development and operation of the systems will take place in Strasbourg.
The cost-effectiveness ratio is very balanced, and this copremi solution in no way calls into question the centralized management of IT systems. Thank you, Madam President. Thank you.
Mr. President, the horizontal social clause and Article 14 of the Treaty of Lisbon guarantee social rights in a binding manner. And in particular, the right of access to services of general interest.
It is time to prove to European citizens that social Europe is on the move.

We must therefore give our social achievements the full place they deserve in community law, by recognizing the specific nature of SGEI in light of competition rules, knowing that these services have strong local roots and rely on social economy actors.

Mr. de Roussin's report is an opportunity to affirm the principles that are simple but strong and meaningful: equality, universal access to these services, organized collectively, which are factors of well-being and social progress.
Madam Commissioner, you said, it is time to get concrete.
Yes, it's time to move on to concrete matters. So, in this debate, I propose that we genuinely support a European legislative instrument for public services of a social nature.
I therefore invite you to vote on the alternative resolution submitted by the Green group.
Thank you, Mr. President. Debates concerning GMOs must imperatively be based on irrefutable arguments, both from a scientific and a legal perspective, for that matter.

The rapporteur has broadened the range of margins of A-flat.
of the scientific basis, which must also be irrefutable.
The council has on several occasions challenged the scientific evaluations of EFSA, which, moreover, are always positive regarding GMOs, contrary to national scientific evaluations.

To restore confidence, shouldn't we first, Mr. Commissioner, simply apply the existing legislation which aims to provide quite a few scientific guarantees, particularly in terms of long-term impact assessment?
Why have the recommendations of the Council of October 2008 remained a dead letter?
Thank you, Madam President, Madam Commissioner. First of all, I would like to thank our colleague Simon Busuttil for the tremendous work he has accomplished.
These reflections have enriched the initial proposal, and we can only commend it.
This report outlines an agency with greatly increased resources, more in line with the missions assigned to it.
Recent events have demonstrated to us the necessity of closer cooperation between the European Union, the Member States, and the support of a strong agency.
Frontex finally obtains the means to fulfill its missions.
By being able to acquire or lease its own equipment and by benefiting from the services of its own border guards, it becomes more independent and very effective.
New tasks are also assigned to it, such as the organization of voluntary returns or the processing of personal data, so that we can fight cross-border crime, while protecting fundamental rights.

This extension of Frontex's scope of competence gives us the means to consider and address migration issues through a global approach, and not to confine ourselves to a simple mechanical management of borders.
I approve the possibility of exchanging personal data with other European agencies, notably Europol.
Finally, the human dimension has taken its full place in this report.
A strong recognition of human rights is guaranteed by the proposal to establish a consultative council within the agency.

I can only welcome this comprehensive proposal which takes a step forward towards a sound and responsible management of our external borders, in a context where migratory pressures are particularly strong and continuous. I thank you.

Thank you, Mr. President. I would like to thank the rapporteur, Mr. Meier, and the comments from my colleagues, sometimes critical, but this 2010 activity report is finally a wonderful opportunity to talk about the Committee on Petitions, because it remains too little known.
Yet, it is the Commission that has a direct, sometimes physical, link with the citizen, who can himself attend our debates.
She has undeniable legitimacy in parliament, as well as extensive expertise, thanks to her direct relationship with the citizen.
Indeed, any petitioner can go to Parliament and defend their rights before the Committee on Petitions.
We can also acknowledge the serenity of the debates despite the painful cases that are often dealt with.
This commission represents Europe as it should be, listening to the problems of its citizens, and united to find solutions and protect them.
Unfortunately, the means of action and the means to exert influence still remain limited, particularly regarding the environmental risk of projects sometimes almost completed.
But I also observe it when it comes to defending specific cases, such as family rights or children's rights in cases of divorce.
We will also be carrying out a mission in November on this subject.
I also regret that national authorities are not always quick to provide us with all the necessary information.
I would like to thank the European Commission for the research and verification work it carries out, even if sometimes it could go further in the search for truth.
I must also acknowledge the constructive work that we are carrying out with the European mediator.
Finally, I appeal to the citizens. Continue to solicit us through petitions, as directly elected representatives.
Our role will always be to preserve your interests in strict compliance with European law.
Madam President, Mr. Commissioner, I welcome this resolution on homelessness, because it is our responsibility to recall its extreme urgency.
Let us first note that what was a priority yesterday remains even more pressing today.

The absence of reliable definitions and statistical indicators on homelessness cannot prevent us from noting, given the current crisis, that the number of people living below the poverty line has risen, according to Eurostat, from 79 million in 2009 to an estimated 84 million in 2011.
Dear colleagues, three years ago, our Parliament, concerned with eradicating poverty, asked member states to put an end to the problem of homelessness by 2015.
Today, we ourselves are somewhat closer to our objective.
Has the year 2010, dedicated to the fight against poverty, truly been put to good use by member states to concretely tackle this great precariousness whose urgency has become permanent?
I genuinely doubt it and I strongly deplore it.

To conclude, I would like to emphasize the need to develop a global strategy, a global multisectoral strategy for homelessness, because of course, there is poverty and lack of housing, certainly, but there are also family problems, health issues, various addictions, or even personal vulnerability.
We need a global, effective, ambitious, but tailored approach that takes into account individuals, the heterogeneous nature of their journeys, and their life disruptions.
This is a right, at the very least fundamental, of which many highly precarious individuals are deprived.

and a half minutes. Thank you, Madam President. In recent years, as everyone has said, the European Union has been hit by unprecedented catastrophes, and listing them here would take up all the all the time of the debate."
I will still mention the AZF explosion, whose 10th anniversary we commemorated less than a week ago in Toulouse.
A strengthened European response, as proposed in Mrs. Gardini's report, represents a real opportunity to demonstrate solidarity among our populations.

And for several years now, the Union has been trying to make operational this pooling of our efforts, and it is now time to act together and no longer to wait for a new catastrophe to strike our territories to draw the lessons from it.
Today, the aim is to make our material, human, and financial capabilities available to everyone, in order to prevent new tragedies from occurring.
Madam Commissioner, earlier, you said that it was necessary to anticipate the risks.

And it is truly regrettable that, in this context, the European Commission is considering withdrawing from funding the GMES satellite observation program, which is universally recognized as an essential instrument for the prevention and coordination of disaster responses.
And I urgently call on the Commission to review this position and to support these flagship programs, which provide Europe with an undeniable added value.
Well, I would like to thank Ms. Gardini for her excellent report.
I hope that we will adopt it; it will thus pave the way for European solidarity in the service of the citizen. Thank you.
Thank you, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Minister. The number of poor and unemployed continues to increase within the European Union,
more than 43 million Europeans are threatened with food poverty.
The situation is serious.

Today, six Member States, six Member States want to put an end to the food aid program without any replacement solution. Germany, Sweden, Denmark, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, and the Czech Republic.
Certainly, there is a decision from the Court of Justice and we must respect it.
However, Mr. Commissioner, Parliament addressed you last July, at the initiative of our Green group, and you made pertinent proposals to safeguard the program.
The Greens are ready to work with you on long-term solutions. But given that millions of people depend on this system, we cannot allow the food aid budget to be cut by three-quarters.
The urgency is real.

If nothing is done by the end of the year, more than 300 million euros will be missing for food banks, and there is no guarantee that national governments will be able to take over to cover this shortfall.
It is therefore a real question of solidarity between peoples.
We are not letting an irresponsible government minority deprive millions of Europeans of food aid, because it is above all a question of survival.
The fight against poverty and malnutrition also requires a reform of the CAP.

We must fundamentally change our way of producing in order to bring food production closer to the populations, especially the poorest who are and who also have the right to a healthy and balanced diet.
Only a truly sustainable CAP will enable us to ensure that fewer Europeans will need food aid.
The Council must return to reason and allow those in need to access this vital aid.
I am speaking specifically to the future government of Andorra, which will have the responsibility of ensuring the presidency of the European Union starting in January.
Return to reason. Do we need hunger riots in Europe to realize the imperative need to change our approach?

When it comes to saving banks, the Council found solutions. But when it comes to saving food banks, that's it, there's no one left. Parliament sent a strong message last time: human beings are more important than finance.
finance, it is high time to reaffirm it.
In any case, we will be very vigilant and we will not fail to renew our appeal in a constructive manner.
Six member states hold in their hands the fate of 18 million Europeans in need.
If six member states who claim to be doing nothing more and nothing less than legalism.
Legalism is one thing, humanism and political choices are another.
How can we not express our anger towards those who remain deaf to the cries of alarm issued by the associations that help the most deprived?
And we, European deputies, representing all citizens, do we want a Europe where national egoisms dictate policies, disregarding the nationals of states in budgetary difficulty?
No question of renationalizing this...

No. Let's maintain this program. Let's give it a stronger legal basis, but let's not abandon those who, right now, in 2012, are counting on this aid during particularly difficult times of crisis. Thank you.

Thank you, Mr. President, Commissioner.
I will not dwell on the cynicism with which the ArcelorMittal group announced the closure of some of its facilities in Europe, particularly in Belgium, in Liège, ultimately causing hundreds of direct redundancies and endangering several thousand indirect jobs.
As a resident of Liège, I share the stupefaction and distress of the 581 dismissed workers and their families.

And does not treat the offenses in this matter lightly.
At the initiative of its government, identity theft is an offense punishable by one year in prison and a fine of €15,000.
The conditions for issuing identity documents have been simplified, which has allowed prefectures to free up additional resources to combat this fraud.
Thus, in each prefecture, a fraud referent is designated.
Moreover, the security provisions for issuing identity documents have been strengthened.
For example, civil status data provided can be verified between administrative services, as much as possible by dematerialized means, in order to limit the risks of falsification.
Some birth certificates are also issued on secure paper.
The same rigor applies to proof of residence.
The Ministry of the Interior has committed to enhanced security for proof of address documents by incorporating a barcode.

The classic processing of a title request includes the authorized consultation of useful files, the comparison of all documents produced during previous title requests, and the eventual authentication of supporting documents.

The security of the issuance of titles is also guaranteed by the double appearance of the applicant first at the time of filing the application, and then at the time of withdrawal of the title, as well as the collection of two digital fingerprints.
And that's without mentioning the reinforced verifications for the renewal of lost and stolen documents for comparative purposes with the data held by the prefectures, I conclude.

Furthermore, France informs international cooperation bodies, and notably Interpol—I am finishing, Mr. President, I have almost finished—of passports issued, lost, or stolen so that they are not used for fraudulent purposes.
